text: The video shows a robotic simple prostatectomy procedure. A transverse cystotomy is performed, with an incision opening the bladder. The catheter balloon is visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon places retraction sutures through the anterior abdominal wall, pulling back the anterolateral aspects of the bladder wall incision to improve exposure.

The surgeon enters the bladder and makes a circumferential incision to outline the removal area of the BPH adenoma. A dissection plane is created between the peripheral and transition zones of the prostate using sharp and blunt dissection techniques. The dissection progresses from the posterior plane to the lateral sides and continues anteriorly, facilitating the excision of the large obstructing benign prostatic adenoma.
The primary surgeon and assistant coordinate their actions, with the assistant using a retraction tool and a suction device to manage the surgical field during the prostatectomy.

The surgeon follows the anterior plane down towards the apex of the prostate using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection. Perforating blood vessels are controlled as they are visualized during the dissection. The dissection continues until the intraprostatic urethra is identified beyond the adenoma, which is then excised and removed, revealing a large central defect within the central cavity of the prostate.

The surgeon inserts a flow seal and a ratech device to provide tamponade for hemostasis. Reconstruction begins with a V-lock suture, fashioning the posterior wall of the anastomosis by starting the suture line at the bladder component and taking bites of the urethra.

The surgeon continues the anastomosis from the posterior aspect of the urethra, progressing through the lateral and anterior aspects of the incision to close the defect. Care is taken to identify and preserve the ureteric orifices. A dissolvable V-lock suture is used, followed by a second suture that incorporates the anterior wall of the urethra and extends laterally to the patient's left.

The surgeon closes the central defect within the prostate using an absorbable V-lock suture, achieving hemostasis. An indwelling catheter is inserted, and the transverse cystotomy is closed with the same type of suture.

The surgeon closes the peritoneum over the previously closed cystotomy site. A leak test is performed to visually confirm the integrity of the anastomosis, ensuring no persistent defects. The procedure is then completed.
